Fnrn Por-rrrcAL PnecrrcES CouMrssroN
"128 J Street o Suite 610 r Sacramenro. CA 9581.1-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) -122-0886
October L,2009
David Kaneda
City of Cupertino
Planning Commission
1084 Foxworthy Ave., Suite 150
San Jose, CA 95118
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-215
Dear Mr. Kaneda:
This letter responds to yolrr request for advice regarding the honorarium provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act'l).' Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions
of the Act. We offer no opinion on the application of any other bodies of law or any
incompatible activities restrictions your agency may impose.
QUESTTONS
l. Are payments, including payments for out-of-state travel and accommodations, made
by a building construction related firm for you to discuss green electrical engineering before the
firm prohibited honoraria?
2. Are payments, including free admission and payments for your travel and
accommodations, made by various organizations for you to give presentations at local and
national conferences regarding green/sustainable design ideas and case studies ofprojects by
your electrical engineering firm prohibited honoraria?
3. Are payments, including payments for your travel, made by the United States General
Services Administration's (the "GSA"), for your assistance in selecting a design team and
reviewing the design of a federal project prohibited honoraria?
I The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
referencesaretotheGovernmentCode.unlessotherwiseindicated. TheregulationsoftheRiirpolitical practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8 I l0 through I ti997 of Title 2 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regutations, unless otherwise indicated.
*i:&j


File No. A-09-215
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
l. Payments made by a building construction firm for you to discuss green electrical
engineering before the firm are earned income for services in connection with a bona fide
profession. Accordingly, these payments are not prohibited honoraria. However, these
payments are income and must be reported under the Act's disclosure provisions.
2. Payments made by various organizations for you to give presentations at local and
national conferences regarding green/sustainable design ideas and case studies ofprojects by
your electrical engineering firm are earned income for services in connection wittra bona fide
profession. Accordingly, these payments are not prohibited honoraria. While these payments
are generally considered income and must be reported under the dct's disclosure prwiiions,
certain payments may not be reportable income. For instance, free admission, payments for
intrastate travel, and necessary lodging in connection with a presentation are not considered
honoraria or reportable income or gifts under Regulations 18932.4(e) and 18950.3. Additionally,
salary and reimbursements for expenses or per diem received from a state, local, or federal
government agency and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a
501(c)(3) nonprofit organization are not considered income subject to reporting under Section
82030.
3. Payments made by the GSA for your assistance in selecting a design team and
reviewing the design of a federal project are earned income for services in connection with a
bona fide profession. Accordingly, these payments are not prohibited honoraria. Moreover,
because these payments are from a federal government agency, these payments are not
considered reportable income under Section 82030.
f,.ACTS
You are a planning commissioner for the City of Cupertino, a small business owner, and
a licensed electrical engineer. Your small business, Integrated Design Associates Inc.
("IDEAS"), is an award winning sustainable electrical engineering and lighting design firm
focusing on the energy efficient design of buildings. The firm specializeJ in cuttingiage
sustainable design including power distribution, lighting design, day-lighting design, relnewable
energy, and low voltage systems. You state that IDEAS is an industry leadei in gieen design
with a large portfolio of Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design and Net ZeroEnirgy
Building projects.
As the owner of IDEAS and specialist in the field of sustainable electrical engineering,
you are regularly asked to speak on business and design topics by various organizations and
groups. Many of these organizations and groups provide payments for the speaking
engagements and reiinbursement of travel expenses. You seek advice relating to the following
three potential speaking engagements:


File No. 4-09-215
Page No. 3
Scenario I
You have been asked to discuss "green electrical engineering" to a building construction
1e.la1ed firm. The speaking engagement is in another state and the firm tras offered to provide the
flight, accommodations, and an honorarium for your presentation.
S
2
You, and other IDEAS engineers, regularly give presentations at local and national
conferences including, but not limited to, the following: AIA National Convention, Greenbuild
Expo National Convention, Lightfair lnternational, PG&E Pacific Energy Center, Coalition for
Adequate School Housing, California Green Schools Summit, and variJus Colleges and
Universities. Presentations typically cover green/sustainable design ideas as well as case studies
of projects by IDEAS. Some presentations are conducted in conjuncture with other design firms
that work together with IDEAS. Frequently, the organization, ,ponroring a conference will offer
free admission to the event, reimbursement for travil and accommodations, and a stipend or
honorarium for the presentation.
Scenario 3
As part of your duty for the GSA National Peer Design Excellence program, you have
been asked to help select a design team and review the design of a federal projlct. These
proceedings typically take 2-4 business days over the course of a few *orihg For your time,
GSA offers to provide an honorarium, daily stipend, and travel expenses.
AI\ALYSIS
Section 89502(a) provides that an "individual specified in Section 87ZOO shall not accept
any honorarium'" As a planning commissioner, you *L an individual specified in Section g7200
and may not accept an honorarium as defined by the Act.
Section 89501 defines the term "honorarium," in pertinent part, as follows:
"(a) For purposes of this chapter, 'honorarium' means, except as provided
in subdivision (b), any payment made in consideration for ory ,p"""h given,
article published, or attendance at any public or private conference, coivention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering.
"(b) The term 'honorarium' does not include:
"(l) Earned income for personal services which are customarily provided
in connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profeision, such
as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or building
contracting, unless the sole or predominant activity of the business,lrade, or


File No. 4-09-215
Page No. 4
profession is making speeches. The Commission shall adopt regulations to
implement this subdivision."
Interpreting Section 8950 I (bX I ), Regulario n 18932 provides :
"(a) 'Honorarium' does not include income earned from personal services
if:
"(1) The services are provided in connection with an individual's business
or the individual's practice of or employment in a bona fide business, trade, or
profession, such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate,
banking, or building contracting, pursuant to [Regulations 18932.1 through
18932.31; and
"(2) The services are customarily provided in connection with the
business, trade, or profession."
Nevertheless, the "eamed income" exception to the honoraria ban does not apply if the
predominant activity of the profession is making speeches. (Section 89501(b)(l).) For a
profession in existence for one year or more, speechmaking is presumed to be the predominant
activity of the profession if during the l2-month period prior to and including the date of the
speech, more than 50 percent of the hours spent on the profession is devotedio, or more than 50
percent of the gross income from the profession is derived from, the preparation or delivery of
speeches. (Regulation 18932.3(a).)
Under the facts you have provided, you are a licensed electrical engineer and own an
electrical engineering firm with a large portfolio of energy efficient buildings. Absent additional
facts, there is no indication that the predominate activity of your firm is speichmaking.
Accordingly, it appears that the eamed income exception will apply so long as your firm is a
"bona fide" business, trade, or profession and the activities in which you *hi"Lto engage are
services "customarily provided" in connection with the business, trade, or profession.
As provided in Regulation 18932.1(c)(1), a trade or professional practitioner is presumed
to be "bona fide" if "the tradesperson or professional practitioner possesies current licensure or
certification which is issued by state or federal occupational licensing authority, the issuance of
which requires a demonstration of skill and knowledge in connection with theielevant trade or
profession." Undoubtedly, your electrical engineering firm is a "bona fide" business, trade, or
profession.
Tuming to the determination of whether the activities in which you wish to engage are
services "customarily provided" in connection with the business, trade, or profession,ihi.
determination must be made on a case-by-case basis. (See Donald Advicqletter, No. A-00-
276.) However, we have previously provided the following advice related to this determination:


File No. A-09-215
Page No. 5
o Proceeds received by a public official, who was also a medical professional and had
written over 300 articles and three textbooks, for publishing books/articles was ..earned
income" and not considered honoraria if the books/articles were published as part of the
official's medical/professional activities. (Donatd Advice Letter, supra.)
o The earned income exception applies to lectures given by a physician to health- care
professionals related to areas of medicine in which he practicei and does research.
(Phillips Advice Letter, No. 4-91-137.) lncontrast, a speech regarding healthcare to a
group of companies that manufacture pharmaceuticals, diagnostic equilment, and various
medical supplies does not appear to be the type of service that is cusiomarily provided in
connection with the practice of medicine. (Filante Advice Letter, A-gz-l4i.t
o Payments received for conducting church activities and speaking as a licensed minister at
youth camps, couples retreats, weddings, and funerals are not p-t ibit"a honoraria.
(Boatwrighr Advice Letter, No. 4-93-323.) However, the exception is limited to
speaking engagements within the scope of the minister's duties and does not apply to
speeches unconnected to that activity. (Metcalf Advice Letter, No. 4-95-316.i - -
Scenario I
You have been invited to provide a presentation on green electrical engineering before a
building construction related firm. So long as this presentation is made solely-to "*ploy.". o,
officers of the firm, this presentation would not be a "speech given" because ihe Act defines
"speech given" as "a public address, oration, or other form of oral presentation.', (Regulation
18931.1, emphasis added.) However, payments for this presentation are still potentialy
prohibited.P{ m" honorarium ban, as an improper payment for your attendance at a private
meeting', if the payments are for services not customarily provided by your profession.
As an electrical engineer and the owner of a firm specializing in green electrical
engineering, a presentation before a building constructionielated firm regarding green electrical
engineering falls within the scope of activity reasonably expected from y-our eniineering firm.
Accordingly, it appears that this presentation is a service cuitomarily prlvided 6y you,
profession, and a payment for the presentation shall be considered earned income and not a
prohibited honorarium.
Scenario 2
You are regularly invited to speak at local and national conferences hosted by various
organizations regarding green/sustainable design ideas and case studies of projects by IDEAS.
As noted above, the determination of whether any particular speech given is .,lustomarily
provided" in connection with your bona fide profession must be madi on a case-by-case-basis.
. 
2 For purposes ofthe honorarium ban, "'attendance' means being present during, making an appearance at,
or serving as host or master ofceremonies for, any public or private confeience, convention, meeting, social event,
meal, or like gathering." (Regulation 18931.3.)


File No. A-09-215
Page No. 6
However, considering your particular qualifications in the field of green electrical engineering,
we can more generally determine that technical presentations regardirrg green/sustairobl" design
ideas and case studies of projects by IDEAS provided at local and national conferences
conducted by organizations and groups with an interest in this field are services customarily
provided by your profession. Accordingly, payments for these speaking engagements shall be
considered earned income and not prohibited honoraria.
Scenario 3
As part of your duty for the GSA, you have been asked to help select a design team and
review the design of a federal project. This proceeding should tuqe i-+ business da=ys over the
course of a few months. Because the honorarium ban ixtends to payments for an official,s
attendance at a meeting under Section 89501(a), payments to attenda meeting of the GSA to
select a design team and review the design of a federal project are potentially prohibited by the
honorarium ban if the payments are for services not cusiomarily provided by your profession.
However, considering your particular qualifications in the fieldoi green eteltiical engineering, it
appears that your work for the GSA is a service customarily providid by your profession, unJu
payment for this service shall be considered earned income and not u p-t iUit"i honorarium.
Notwithstanding the conclusion that the payments you have described appear to be
earned income and not prohibited honoraria, the payments may be income subject to disclosure
under the Act's disclosure provisions.3 lsee Seciions 87200 et seq.) However, certain payments
may not be reportable income under other provisions of the Act. For instance, salary and 
-
reimbursements for expenses or per diem received from a state, local, or federal government
agency and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a 50licX3) nonprofit
organization are not considered income subject to reporting under Section 82030. In addition,
Regulations 18932.4(e) and 18950.3 provide that the following are not considered honoraria or
reportable income or gifts:
"Free admission, and refreshments and similar non-cash nominal benefits
provided to a filer during the entire event at which the filer gives a speech,
participates in a panel or seminar, or provides a similar r"*i"", and actual
intrastate transportation and any necessary lodging and subsistence provided
directly in connection with the speech, panel, r"mirrar, or service, including but
not limited to meals and beverages on the day of the activity....,'a
Finally, we note that certain payments in connection with a speech may be permitted
even if the payments do not fall within the earned income exception to the honorarium ban.
3 We note that your economic interest in a source of income may give rise to a conflict of interest
disqualifying you from making, participating in making, or using yor. porit-ion to influence a governmental decision
if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable moteiial t:nanciat ef'fbct on the source of the income.
a "Necessary lodging" is generally limited to the day of the speech, but may include the day befbre or after,
if necessary due to travel arangements . (Allen Advice tettei, No. l-s4-zzo.)


File No. 4-09-215
Page No. 7
Regulation 18950.1 provides exceptions to the Act's prohibitions or limitations on honorarium or
gifts for payments made for travel that are reasonably related to a legislative or governmental .,
purpose' or to an issue of state, national, or international public if thi travel is (i within the
United States and in connection with a speech given by the official or (2)paid ior by a
govemmental entity, certain educational institutions, or certain nonprofit organizations.
However, in light of the conclusion that the payments you have described uf,p"* to be earned
income and not prohibited honoraria, it is unnecessary for us to further "onrii", 
these exceptions.
If you need further assistance related to payments other than the ones presented in your request
for advice, it is advisable that you seek additional advice providing alirelevant facts.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGLjgl

firm prohibited honoraria?
2. Are payments, including free admission and payments for your travel and
accommodations, made by various organizations for you to give presentations at local and

CONCLUSION: l. Payments made by a building construction firm for you to discuss green electrical
engineering before the firm are earned income for services in connection with a bona fide
profession. Accordingly, these payments are not prohibited honoraria. However, these
payments are income and must be reported under the Act's disclosure provisions.
2. Payments made by various organizations for you to give presentations at local and
national conferences regarding green/sustainable design ideas and case studies ofprojects by
your electrical engineering firm are earned income for services in connection wittra bona fide
profession. Accordingly, these payments are not prohibited honoraria. While these payments
are generally considered income and must be reported under the dct's disclosure prwiiions,
certain payments may not be reportable income. For instance, free admission, payments for
intrastate travel, and necessary lodging in connection with a presentation are not considered
honoraria or reportable income or gifts under Regulations 18932.4(e) and 18950.3. Additionally,
salary and reimbursements for expenses or per diem received from a state, local, or federal
government agency and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a
501(c)(3) nonprofit organization are not considered income subject to reporting under Section
82030.
3. Payments made by the GSA for your assistance in selecting a design team and
reviewing the design of a federal project are earned income for services in connection with a
bona fide profession. Accordingly, these payments are not prohibited honoraria. Moreover,
because these payments are from a federal government agency, these payments are not
considered reportable income under Section 82030.
f,.ACTS
You are a planning commissioner for the City of Cupertino, a small business owner, and
a licensed electrical engineer. Your small business, Integrated Design Associates Inc.
("IDEAS"), is an award winning sustainable electrical engineering and lighting design firm
focusing on the energy efficient design of buildings. The firm specializeJ in cuttingiage
sustainable design including power distribution, lighting design, day-lighting design, relnewable
energy, and low voltage systems. You state that IDEAS is an industry leadei in gieen design
with a large portfolio of Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design and Net ZeroEnirgy
Building projects.
As the owner of IDEAS and specialist in the field of sustainable electrical engineering,
you are regularly asked to speak on business and design topics by various organizations and
groups. Many of these organizations and groups provide payments for the speaking
engagements and reiinbursement of travel expenses. You seek advice relating to the following
three potential speaking engagements:
Scenario I
You have been asked to discuss "green electrical engineering" to a building construction
1e.la1ed firm. The speaking engagement is in another state and the firm tras offered to provide the
flight, accommodations, and an honorarium for your presentation.
S
2
You, and other IDEAS engineers, regularly give presentations at local and national
conferences including, but not limited to, the following: AIA National Convention, Greenbuild
Expo National Convention, Lightfair lnternational, PG&E Pacific Energy Center, Coalition for
Adequate School Housing, California Green Schools Summit, and variJus Colleges and
Universities. Presentations typically cover green/sustainable design ideas as well as case studies
of projects by IDEAS. Some presentations are conducted in conjuncture with other design firms
that work together with IDEAS. Frequently, the organization, ,ponroring a conference will offer
free admission to the event, reimbursement for travil and accommodations, and a stipend or
honorarium for the presentation.
Scenario 3
As part of your duty for the GSA National Peer Design Excellence program, you have
been asked to help select a design team and review the design of a federal projlct. These
proceedings typically take 2-4 business days over the course of a few *orihg For your time,
GSA offers to provide an honorarium, daily stipend, and travel expenses.

Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We offer no opinion on the application of any other bodies of law or any incompatible activities restrictions your agency may impose. QUESTTONS l. Are payments, including payments for out-of-state travel and accommodations, made by a building construction related firm for you to discuss green electrical engineering before the firm prohibited honoraria? 2. Are payments, including free admission and payments for your travel and accommodations, made by various organizations for you to give presentations at local and national conferences regarding green/sustainable design ideas and case studies ofprojects by your electrical engineering firm prohibited honoraria? 3. Are payments, including payments for your travel, made by the United States General Services Administration's (the "GSA"), for your assistance in selecting a design team and reviewing the design of a federal project prohibited honoraria? Payments made by a building construction firm for you to discuss green electrical engineering before the firm are earned income for services in connection with a bona fide profession. Payments made by various organizations for you to give presentations at local and national conferences regarding green/sustainable design ideas and case studies ofprojects by your electrical engineering firm are earned income for services in connection wittra bona fide profession.
